narrative: Dear [Student's Name], imagine the admissions team looking at your application not just as a collection of numbers, but as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer—and unfortunately, the chapter they saw raised some real concerns about your chances of passing the bar exam. Your LSAT score of 26 stood out as the biggest red flag, whispering to them that you might struggle with the intense reasoning demands of the bar, more than any other part of your profile. This was echoed by your Year 1 decile of 1.0, suggesting an early rocky start in law school that often sets a tough path ahead. Your Year 3 decile of 1.0 reinforced that pattern, painting a picture of consistent challenges in keeping up with the pack. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.4 and upper-middle family income, those didn't quite counterbalance the hurdles, as the model saw them pulling in the same worrying direction. Your background as a Black female full-time student added layers to this story, with most elements tilting toward higher risks of not making it through the bar. The one bright note was your gender, which nudged things slightly toward success in the model's view, like a gentle tailwind. Together, these pieces added up to a 71.6% predicted chance of bar failure, heavy enough to pause your application. It's not that your passion or potential isn't there—it's that this specific blend has tripped up others before. If you boost that LSAT or show stronger early grades next time, your story could shift dramatically. You've got the drive; now it's about reshaping the narrative. We're rooting for you to rewrite this ending.
